body_txt: Tropey , stop biting that! You're going to pull out the stitches! I mean it! C'mere you! In veterinary practice, the Elizabethan Collar (also known colloquially as an "e-collar," "lampshade," note Not related to Lampshade Hanging , by the way. "cone of shame," or "pet radar dish") has the purpose of promoting healing of a wound or lesion by preventing the animal from messing with it. It's very effective. However, there is one big problem with this device... It looks absolutely ridiculous . These devices often take the form of a large white plastic cone, though a variety of alternatives to this design are on the market. The typical "funnel" design is preferred in fiction because it's... well, funny . Not only does it make the wearer look pathetic and reduces their peripheral vision, it also opens the door for slapstick gags like bouncing off chairs, catching objects and being unable to reach them, getting stuck in the pet door, or simply battering their owners' shins ( sometimes on purpose ). Just like neutering , it is one of the fates that animals despise. If the show features talking pets or funny animals , expect this to pop up at least once. Not to be confused with Dunce Cap . If you are looking for actual Elizabethan collars (i.e. those worn by nobility in the 16th century), see Regal Ruff . (Funnily enough, "Ruff" is a great and not-too-uncommon dog name, sounding as it does like a bark.)
